ucs.title: [LLM] GOLD SOUTHFIELD (Pinchy Spider) Kaseya-style REvil deployment via MSP/RMM agent abuse
ucs.description: Hunts the signature execution chain GOLD SOUTHFIELD used in the July 2021 Kaseya VSA supply-chain compromise and other MSP intrusions: a trusted RMM/MSP agent process (AgentMon.exe, ScreenConnect, ConnectWise) spawning cmd.exe to disable Defender real-time monitoring, run a long ping sleep, and use certutil to decode an embedded REvil payload. This sequence is unique to Pinchy Spider's affiliate playbook and was the exact procedure delivered to ~1,500 downstream Kaseya VSA customers.
ucs.splunk: | tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(Processes.process) as cmdlines values(Processes.process_name) as procs from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where (Processes.parent_process_name IN ("AgentMon.exe","ScreenConnect.ClientService.exe","ConnectWiseControl.Client.exe","kworking*.exe")) AND Processes.process_name IN ("cmd.exe","powershell.exe","certutil.exe") AND (Processes.process="*ping*-n*" OR Processes.process="*Set-MpPreference*-DisableRealtimeMonitoring*" OR Processes.process="*-DisableIntrusionPreventionSystem*" OR Processes.process="*certutil*-decode*agent.crt*" OR Processes.process="*copy*\\windows\\cert.exe*") by host Processes.user Processes.parent_process_name Processes.process_name Processes.process | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | eventstats dc(process_name) as proc_diversity by host | where proc_diversity>=2 | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)`
ucs.kql:
let suspicious_parents = dynamic(["AgentMon.exe","ScreenConnect.ClientService.exe","ScreenConnect.WindowsClient.exe","ConnectWiseControl.Client.exe"]);
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(30d)
| where InitiatingProcessFileName in~ (suspicious_parents) or InitiatingProcessParentFileName in~ (suspicious_parents) or InitiatingProcessFolderPath has @"\kworking\"
| where FileName in~ ("cmd.exe","powershell.exe","certutil.exe")
| where ProcessCommandLine has_any ("Set-MpPreference -DisableRealtimeMonitoring","DisableIntrusionPreventionSystem","DisableScriptScanning","certutil -decode","agent.crt","ping 127.0.0.1 -n 5","copy /Y C:\\Windows\\System32\\certutil.exe C:\\Windows\\cert.exe")
| summarize CmdChain=make_set(ProcessCommandLine,10), Procs=make_set(FileName), FirstSeen=min(Timestamp), LastSeen=max(Timestamp) by DeviceName, AccountName, InitiatingProcessFileName
| where array_length(Procs) >= 2 or CmdChain has_any ("agent.crt","-DisableRealtimeMonitoring")
| extend Severity = iff(CmdChain has "agent.crt", "High", "Medium")
ucs.rationale: GOLD SOUTHFIELD's affiliates do not just drop ransomware — they specifically abuse trusted MSP/RMM agents (the Kaseya VSA chain wrote agent.crt + agent.exe to C:\kworking\ and used AgentMon.exe to invoke a cmd.exe one-liner combining ping-sleep, Set-MpPreference, and certutil -decode). The conjunction of (RMM parent) + (Defender tamper) + (certutil decode of a .crt) is essentially a fingerprint of

ucs.title: [LLM] REvil/Sodinokibi DLL side-loading via vulnerable MsMpEng.exe loading mpsvc.dll
ucs.description: Hunts GOLD SOUTHFIELD's signature evasion technique: dropping an outdated, signed Microsoft Defender binary (MsMpEng.exe, ~2014 build) into a writable directory alongside a malicious mpsvc.dll, then executing it so the legitimate AV process side-loads the REvil payload. This abuses a Microsoft-signed LOLBIN to bypass EDR ML models and AppLocker — a documented Pinchy Spider/REvil hallmark.
ucs.splunk: | tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(Processes.process_path) as paths values(Processes.parent_process_name) as parents values(Processes.process) as cmdline from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.process_name="MsMpEng.exe" AND NOT (Processes.process_path="*\\Windows Defender\\*" OR Processes.process_path="*\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Platform\\*" OR Processes.process_path="*\\ProgramData\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\*") by host Processes.user Processes.process_path Processes.parent_process_name | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | join type=left host [| tstats summariesonly=true count as vss_delete from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.process_name IN ("vssadmin.exe","wmic.exe","bcdedit.exe") AND (Processes.process="*shadow*delete*" OR Processes.process="*shadowcopy*delete*" OR Processes.process="*recoveryenabled*No*" OR Processes.process="*bootstatuspolicy*ignoreallfailures*") by host | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)`] | where vss_delete>0 OR isnotnull(paths) | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)`
ucs.kql:
let sideload = DeviceImageLoadEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(30d)
| where InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "MsMpEng.exe"
| where FileName =~ "mpsvc.dll"
| where InitiatingProcessFolderPath !startswith @"C:\Program Files\Windows Defender"
      and InitiatingProcessFolderPath !startswith @"C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows Defender"
      and InitiatingProcessFolderPath !startswith @"C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Security Client"
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, MsMpEngPath=InitiatingProcessFolderPath, DllPath=FolderPath, InitiatingProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessParentFileName;
let recovery_kill = DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(30d)
| where (FileName =~ "vssadmin.exe" and ProcessCommandLine has_all ("shadow","delete"))
     or (FileName =~ "wmic.exe" and ProcessCommandLine has "shadowcopy delete")
     or (FileName =~ "bcdedit.exe" and ProcessCommandLine has_any ("recoveryenabled No","bootstatuspolicy ignoreallfailures"))
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, RecoveryCmd=ProcessCommandLine;
sideload
| join kind=leftouter (recovery_kill) on DeviceName
| extend Severity = iff(isnotempty(RecoveryCmd), "Critical", "High")
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, MsMpEngPath, DllPath, InitiatingProcessCommandLine, RecoveryCmd, Severity
ucs.rationale: Side-loading mpsvc.dll under an old MsMpEng.exe was first publicly documented as a REvil/Sodinokibi (Pinchy Spider) loader and has since been reused across GOLD SOUTHFIELD affiliate intrusions. Legitimate MsMpEng.exe only ever runs from the Defender Platform path; any execution from user-writable paths (Desktop, Temp, ProgramData root, network shares) loading mpsvc.dll is essentially pathognomonic
